description: Vision, speech, image generation, cross-modal embeddings, and multimodal architectures.

questions.question: What distinguishes a 'multimodal' AI model from a text-only language model?
options: It runs on multiple CPUs in parallel rather than on a single GPU
It can process and/or generate more than one type of data, such as text, images, audio, or video
It is trained on text data in multiple human languages simultaneously
It uses multiple separate model checkpoints that vote to produce a final answer
questions.explanation: Multimodal models handle several data modalities — for example, GPT-4o can accept text, images, and audio, and produce text or audio — rather than being limited to a single data type.

questions.question: What is a 'vision-language model' (VLM)?
options: A text-only model fine-tuned on descriptions of visual content written by human annotators
A model that connects visual and linguistic information to perform tasks such as captioning or visual question answering
A model deployed inside augmented-reality glasses to render visual overlays in real time
A model that converts language text into technical 3D scene-graph representations for computer graphics
questions.explanation: Vision-language models learn relationships between visual and language representations so they can handle tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering, and multimodal reasoning. Some use distinct encoders and language components; others are trained more jointly, so one component layout is not part of the definition.

questions.question: In text-to-image models like Stable Diffusion, what role does the 'denoising diffusion' process play?
options: It removes watermarks and compression artifacts from user-uploaded source images
It gradually removes noise from a random latent tensor, guided by a text conditioning signal, to produce a coherent image
It applies successive blur filters to upscale low-resolution images to print quality
It trains the model by randomly corrupting training images and teaching it to restore them without any text signal
questions.explanation: Diffusion models learn to reverse a noise process. At inference, they start from pure noise in a latent space and iteratively denoise, steered at each step by an embedding of the text prompt, until a sharp image emerges.

questions.question: What is CLIP (Contrastive Language–Image Pre-training)?
options: A video editing toolkit that automatically clips and segments long recordings into highlights
A joint embedding model trained to align text and image representations in a shared vector space using contrastive learning
A safety filter that clips and removes explicit visual content from image generation pipelines
A lossless image compression algorithm optimized for neural-network feature maps
questions.explanation: CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) is trained on (image, caption) pairs with a contrastive objective: matching image–text pairs are pulled together in embedding space and non-matching pairs are pushed apart. The resulting embeddings power zero-shot image classification and guide diffusion models.

questions.question: What is 'optical character recognition' (OCR) in the context of multimodal AI?
options: A model skill that detects and corrects character-level spelling errors inside long documents
Extracting readable text from images or scanned documents so downstream models can process it
A method for encoding text characters into optical fiber signals for high-bandwidth transmission
Converting text prompts into stylized calligraphy images using generative diffusion models
questions.explanation: OCR identifies and transcribes text visible in images — receipts, screenshots, PDFs, whiteboards — making pixel-level content machine-readable. Modern VLMs often perform OCR natively without a separate pipeline step.

questions.question: In automatic speech recognition (ASR), what does the model output?
options: A sentiment score and emotional tone analysis of the speaker's vocal patterns
A waveform that replicates the audio in a different voice or accent
A text transcript of the spoken audio input
A MIDI file encoding the pitch and timing of phonemes in the recording
questions.explanation: ASR (speech-to-text) models such as OpenAI Whisper take raw audio waveforms and produce text transcripts. They underpin voice assistants, meeting transcription tools, and voice-controlled agents.

questions.question: What is a 'Vision Transformer' (ViT)?
options: A convolutional neural network augmented with attention gates on the final classification layer
A transformer model that splits an image into fixed-size patches, embeds them as token sequences, and applies self-attention
A large language model fine-tuned exclusively on textual descriptions of visual art styles and scenes
A hardware accelerator chip optimized to run image-processing attention kernels at lower latency
questions.explanation: ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) treats an image as a sequence of non-overlapping patches, linearly projects each patch into an embedding, and feeds the sequence to a Transformer encoder. ViT and related architectures are widely used as visual backbones, but not every VLM uses the same backbone.

questions.question: What is 'cross-modal retrieval'?
options: Searching a text document corpus using another text document as the query
Using an input in one modality (e.g., a text query) to retrieve the most relevant items in a different modality (e.g., images or audio)
Translating a model trained on English text to operate on other languages without retraining
Transferring weights from a vision model into a language model through layer merging
questions.explanation: Cross-modal retrieval exploits shared embedding spaces like CLIP's: a text query can retrieve matching images, or an image can retrieve matching captions, because both modalities are projected into the same vector space where similarity is meaningful.

questions.question: What is the 'image–text alignment' problem in training multimodal models?
options: Ensuring image file formats are compatible with the text tokenizer's byte-encoding scheme
The challenge of learning a shared representation where semantically related images and texts are close in embedding space, despite their fundamentally different raw structures
Making sure that image captions appear at the correct pixel coordinates inside the rendered document layout
Preventing the model from generating text descriptions that do not match the visual style guide
questions.explanation: Images are 2D grids of pixel values; text is a sequence of discrete tokens. Bridging them requires architectures — cross-attention, contrastive projection heads, or Q-Former adapters — that can relate the two structurally dissimilar representations into a common semantic space.

questions.question: What publicly documented design change distinguished GPT-4o from the earlier ChatGPT Voice Mode pipeline?
options: GPT-4o converted every audio and image input to text before passing it to the unchanged GPT-4 model
GPT-4o used a separate speech recognizer, text model, and text-to-speech model, just with lower network latency
GPT-4o was trained end-to-end across text, vision, and audio in one model, whereas the earlier voice experience chained three separate models
GPT-4o's published architecture guaranteed shared attention weights across every modality at every transformer layer
questions.explanation: OpenAI documented GPT-4o as one model trained end-to-end across text, vision, and audio, with the same neural network processing all inputs and outputs. Earlier Voice Mode used a three-model speech-to-text, language-model, and text-to-speech pipeline. OpenAI's announcement did not publish enough internal detail to claim a particular fusion mechanism at every layer.
